what_it_does: Registers an MCP tool named point that accepts an opaque token and returns it; the tool-call itself acts as a presentation signal for the client to spotlight the corresponding on-screen object.
observations: The entire runtime behavior is a single decorated nested function returning {"ok": True, "token": ...}.
The docstring frames the tool as a pure presentation signal with no state change, no address knowledge, and no server-side token validation.
The token is intentionally opaque: this file never maps it to a UI address; mapping is the client's responsibility.
The file references external concepts such as run_turn, window.surfacePointables(), fork-brain-core, and projection's resolveUiTarget sink, but does not implement them.
The implementation coerces the token to a string and accepts empty values without error.
standouts: Extensive interpretive docstring that defines an 'operator-law' and 'Tim's law' to constrain usage.
The tool is deliberately a no-op carrier: the side effect is the tool-call event, not the return value.
Strong separation between the brain/server and UI addressing: addresses never touch this file.
conventions: Uses an MCP decorator (@mcp.tool()) for tool registration.
Nested registration function pattern: register(mcp, suite) defines the tool inside its body.
Type-hinted function signature and dict return value.
Docstring-driven design: behavior and architectural contract are described in the module docstring.
concerns: Token is cast to string and empty or None tokens are accepted silently, which could emit meaningless signals.
There is no server-side logging, validation, or error path, so malformed or stale tokens are invisible to this layer.
The nested function is exposed as register.point, which may be surprising to tooling or static analysis.
notes: A thin, address-agnostic MCP tool whose only job is to broadcast an opaque point token so the client can spotlight the right UI element.
questions: How does the MCP framework surface the tool-call event to the client as {type:'point', token}?
What contract guarantees that the client's token-to-address catalog matches the catalog supplied to the brain?
How are stale or unknown tokens handled at the client/projection layer, and is there any feedback to the server?
What do mcp and suite provide, and how is the tool name and schema derived?
purpose_vs_actual: Matches: the file is described as a read/cosmetic presentation signal and its implementation does nothing beyond returning the token it received.
apparent_themes: Opaque tokens and address-free server logic
Minimal implementation with maximal contract documentation
intention_signals: It appears designed to let the brain gesture at on-screen objects in real time while speaking, without ever knowing or trusting UI addresses, by emitting a token that the client resolves locally.
novelty: The tool intentionally avoids all target resolution: it treats the MCP tool call itself as the spotlight signal and leaves every mapping decision to the client.
contribution: Provides a server-side point verb that accepts an opaque token and returns it, enabling the brain to issue on-screen pointing signals while keeping UI addressing entirely on the client.
summary_for_embedding: mcp_face/tools/point.py registers a minimal MCP point tool that returns an opaque token, using the tool-call event as a presentation signal for client-side UI spotlighting.
intention_for_embedding: To give the brain an address-free way to gesture at on-screen referents during a turn, delegating token-to-UI mapping to the client.

symbols.does: Registers the point MCP tool with the given mcp and suite objects.
symbols.description: The register function is the module's entry point for tool wiring; it accepts mcp and suite arguments and defines the point tool inside its body so the MCP framework can expose it.

symbols.does: Accepts an opaque token and returns it as a presentation signal for client-side spotlighting.
symbols.description: The point tool is a decorated nested function that takes a token string, coerces it to a string, and returns {"ok": True, "token": token}. The tool call itself is the signal; the server performs no validation or address resolution.
